name: Ángel de la Independencia
thenCaption: 1910 — golden angel raised for independence centennial on Reforma.
nowCaption: Today — protest meeting point; surrounded by towers and traffic.
summary: Built for the 1910 centennial, the Ángel crowned Paseo de la Reforma — Díaz's Parisian boulevard. It became the city's default stage for celebration and protest, where political claims are made in full view of finance and traffic.
planningRole: Porfirian monument · Reforma axis · civic gathering point
inequalityAngle: Miguel Hidalgo alcaldía hosts both the Ángel and Polanco's wealth — planning prestige beside extreme inequality.

name: Palacio de Bellas Artes
planner: Adamo Boari; completed by Federico Mariscal
thenCaption: 1934 — marble palace finally finished after revolution interrupted construction.
nowCaption: Today — mural cathedral; metro hub; cultural heart of the historic center.
summary: Started under Porfirio Díaz, interrupted by revolution, finished in 1934 — the Palacio de Bellas Artes embodies Mexico City's layered modernity. Its dome and murals anchor Cuauhtémoc's role as the cultural-political core.
planningRole: Porfirian showcase · post-revolutionary culture · centro renewal
inequalityAngle: World-class culture in an alcaldía with high crowding and mixed income — formal grandeur amid dense popular neighborhoods.

name: Monumento a la Revolución
planner: Carlos Obregón Santacilia; dome of unfinished legislative palace
thenCaption: 1938 — Porfirian parliament shell reborn as revolutionary monument.
nowCaption: Today — plaza, museum, concerts; Tabacalera neighborhood changing fast.
summary: Díaz's planned congressional palace stalled after 1910; in 1938 the dome became a monument to the Revolution. It marks how Mexico City recycles imperial ambitions into nationalist symbols.
inequalityAngle: A monument to popular revolution in a district now attracting creative-class investment and displacement.
creditNow: Wikimedia Commons · Diego Delso, CC BY-SA

name: Torre Latinoamericana
planner: Augusto H. Álvarez; Mexican modernist skyscraper
thenCaption: 1956 — Latin America's tallest tower; survived 1985 earthquake.
nowCaption: Today — observation deck; Eje Central canyon of towers and street trade.
summary: When it opened in 1956, the Torre Latinoamericana was the tallest building in Latin America — proof that Mexico City could compete with Chicago and New York. Its earthquake survival made it a symbol of technical modernity.
planningRole: Modernist skyline · CBD consolidation · vertical centrality
inequalityAngle: Finance and services concentrate in Cuauhtémoc while eastern alcaldías supply commuters who cannot afford to live nearby.

name: Templo Mayor & Zócalo
planner: INAH excavation; colonial plaza over Aztec sacred center
thenCaption: 1978 — electric workers unearth the Aztec pyramid; archaeology rewrites the center.
nowCaption: Today — museum beside the cathedral; protests still fill the Zócalo.
summary: The Zócalo sits atop Tenochtitlan's ceremonial core. Rediscovery of the Templo Mayor in 1978 forced Mexico City to acknowledge indigenous urbanism beneath colonial and Porfirian layers — planning as palimpsest.
planningRole: Colonial plaza · archaeological recovery · indigenous heritage
inequalityAngle: National narrative anchored downtown while peri-urban indigenous migrants live with weakest services in Iztapalapa and beyond.

name: Museo Nacional de Antropología
thenCaption: 1964 — umbrella fountain; Olympic-era showcase of pre-Hispanic Mexico.
nowCaption: Today — Mexico's most visited museum; Chapultepec forest under pressure.
summary: Built for a modernizing capital, the MNA collected Mexico's archaeological heritage under one monumental roof. Ramírez Vázquez's courtyard fountain became an icon of mid-century nationalist planning in green Chapultepec.
planningRole: Olympic-era modernism · park institution · heritage display
inequalityAngle: World-class museum in wealthy Miguel Hidalgo; east-side residents travel far for the same public culture.
creditNow: Wikimedia Commons · Diego Delso, CC BY-SA

name: Tlali (Reforma) · Columbus replaced
nowCaption: 2022 — 'Tlali' honors indigenous women; debates over memory continue.
summary: For decades a Columbus column stood on Reforma as a Porfirian claim to European modernity. In 2022 the city installed Pedro Reyes's *Tlali* — a deliberate re-writing of whose history the boulevard celebrates.
planningRole: Monument politics · decolonial planning · Reforma identity
inequalityAngle: Symbolic justice on the postcard avenue while land inequality in surrounding alcaldías remains among the highest in the metropolis.

name: Ciudad Universitaria (UNAM)
planner: Mario Pani, Enrique del Moral; UNESCO campus
thenCaption: 1954 — volcanic-stone campus; Olympic Stadium; mural integration.
nowCaption: Today — 300k+ students; southern city anchor; long commutes from east.
summary: UNAM's Ciudad Universitaria concentrated higher education in a single modern campus — a planning bet on scale and symbolism. Its murals and stadium made Coyoacán a knowledge pole while millions commute from distant alcaldías.
planningRole: Educational megaproject · modernist campus · south-city pole
inequalityAngle: Coyoacán benefits from university spillovers; students from Iztapalapa ride hours daily — planning access without residential equity.
